claims.label: Definition 4.1 (Canonical segment representative)
claims.statement: Let seg = (g_1, ..., g_n) be a single-qubit segment in application order, and let q_1, ..., q_n be the translated unit-quaternion representatives of those gates. The canonical segment representative is Q(seg) = Can(q_n ... q_1).
claims.notes: This definition introduces the canonical paper-specific object Q(seg) used throughout the trust layer.

claims.label: Definition 4.2 (Authoritative equivalence)
claims.statement: Two single-qubit segments seg and seg' are canonically equivalent, written Verify(seg, seg'), when Q(seg) = Q(seg').
claims.notes: This is the authoritative correctness criterion used by the paper.

claims.label: Proposition 4.3 (Canonical equivalence is exact segment equivalence modulo global phase)
claims.statement: For single-qubit segments, Verify(seg, seg') holds if and only if seg and seg' represent the same SU(2) action up to the global-phase quotient inherited from U(2).
claims.proof_sketch: Single-qubit actions modulo global phase correspond exactly to sign classes {q, -q} of unit quaternions under the fixed map Phi. The canonicalization rule chooses exactly one representative from each sign class, so equality of canonical representatives is equivalent to equality of represented action modulo global phase.
claims.notes: This proposition resolves the trust question for quaternionic optimization directly in the native representation.

claims.label: Definition 4.4 (Quaternionic compilation invariants)
claims.statement: For a valid optimized replacement of a single-qubit segment, the preserved invariants are: unit norm after normalization, canonical sign choice under the fixed hemisphere rule, represented SU(2) action modulo global phase, and reproducible canonical output form.
claims.notes: These are the core invariants used in the verifier.

claims.label: Proposition 4.6 (Canonical output form is deterministic)
claims.statement: If two compilation runs produce valid optimized representations of the same single-qubit segment action under the fixed convention, then their canonical quaternionic payloads are identical.
claims.proof_sketch: Both outputs represent the same sign class {q, -q} in the unit quaternions, and the canonicalization map chooses exactly one representative from that class.
claims.notes: This is the reproducibility guarantee of the trust layer.

claims.label: Definition 5.1 (Canonical validation procedure)
claims.statement: To validate a candidate optimized segment seg' against an original segment seg: translate each segment into the fixed quaternionic representation, multiply in application order to obtain raw segment products, normalize and sign-canonicalize both products, and compare the resulting canonical representatives for equality.
claims.notes: This is the operational trust workflow advocated by the paper.

claims.label: Proposition 6.4 (Canonical equality implies perfect matrix diagnostics)
claims.statement: If Verify(seg, seg') holds exactly, then for the corresponding matrix representatives U and V one has MatrixEq(U, V), ProcFid(U, V) = 1, TraceDiag(U, V) = 2, and PhaseRes(U, V) = 0.
claims.proof_sketch: Canonical equality implies the same unit quaternion representative. Under Phi, the two segments therefore produce the same SU(2) matrix, so U dagger V = I and the stated diagnostics follow immediately.
claims.notes: This proposition ties the authoritative quaternionic criterion to the supporting matrix diagnostics.

claims.label: Proposition 6.1 (Validation of optimized segments)
claims.statement: A local single-qubit optimization is valid when the optimized segment and the original segment have equal canonical segment representatives; matrix diagnostics may be reported in addition but do not override the canonical equivalence result.
claims.proof_sketch: This is a direct consequence of canonical equivalence being sufficient and necessary for equality of represented single-qubit action modulo global phase.
claims.notes: This is the trust decision rule the paper recommends for toolchains.

claims.label: Empirical Result 8.1 (Reference verifier behavior)
claims.statement: On the shipped seven-case reference verification corpus, all four positive equivalence cases pass and all three negative cases are rejected under the canonical quaternion check, with matrix diagnostics consistent with those outcomes.
claims.proof_sketch: Measured by the included verifier script verify_canonical_equivalence.py, with outputs recorded in verification_results.json.
claims.notes: This is a measured consistency result on the shipped verification corpus only.

claims.label: Interpretive Claim 9.1 (Trust layer for quaternionic compilation)
claims.statement: RQM Technologies uses canonical quaternion equality together with secondary matrix diagnostics as the trust layer for quaternionic compilation because that combination yields deterministic native verification and interoperable external reporting without changing the underlying semantics.
claims.notes: This is an organizational trust claim, not a new mathematical theorem.
